title: Septic Shock
oneLiner: A life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection, requiring immediate recognition and aggressive intervention.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
You are bleeped to see a 72-year-old man on the orthopaedic ward, three days post‑hip hemiarthroplasty. The nurse says he “looks off” and his blood pressure is 82/45. On arrival, he is drowsy but rousable, breathing at 28/min, heart rate 112 bpm, temperature 38.9°C, SaO₂ 90% on room air. His right thigh wound is erythematous and weeping serous fluid. Capillary refill is 5 seconds, peripheries are cool. He has been anuric for 6 hours.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Hypotension (82/45)** → inadequate perfusion pressure → septic shock (not just sepsis) – need vasopressors after fluid.
- **Drowsy/confusion** → cerebral hypoperfusion or sepsis-associated encephalopathy.
- **Tachypnoea (28) + hypoxia (90%)** → ARDS or compensatory respiratory alkalosis ± metabolic acidosis.
- **Tachycardia (112)** → compensatory response to vasodilation and hypovolaemia.
- **Fever (38.9°C)** → acute phase response; but note hypothermia can also occur in severe sepsis.
- **Wound infection** → source – likely Gram‑positive (Staph aureus/Strep) or mixed.
- **Prolonged capillary refill, cool peripheries** → peripheral vasoconstriction suggests a “cold” shock pattern (often hypovolaemic or cardiogenic component).
- **Anuria** → acute kidney injury from hypoperfusion.

**Atypical presentations:**
1. **“Warm” septic shock** – young female with pyelonephritis: hypotensive but bounding pulses, warm peripheries, capillary refill <2s (vasoplegic shock early phase).
2. **Hypothermic sepsis** – elderly male with urosepsis: temp 35.5°C, lethargic, no fever – common in immunocompromised or very old.
3. **Occult abdominal source** – post‑op patient with ileus, mild abdominal distension, rising lactate – no obvious peritonitis. E.g., anastomotic leak.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain‑language definition:** Sepsis is your body’s overwhelming inflammatory response to an infection; septic shock is when that response causes blood pressure to drop so low that organs are starved of oxygen – it’s the deadliest form.

**Why it matters:**  
- **Frequency:** >11 million sepsis‑related deaths globally per year; in UK hospitals, ~260,000 cases annually with ~48,000 deaths.
- **Stakes:** Mortality for septic shock ranges 40–60% if not recognised immediately; every hour delay in antibiotics increases mortality by ~8%.
- **Common pitfalls:** Missing the diagnosis in the “warm” phase; fluid‑overloading the patient with poor ventricular function; not escalating to ICU early; failing to re‑source control.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
The story begins with a pathogen (bacteria, virus, fungus, parasite) entering the body. That pathogen triggers pattern‑recognition receptors (Toll‑like receptors, etc.) on immune cells → massive release of pro‑inflammatory cytokines (TNF‑α, IL‑1, IL‑6).

**Why each sign occurs:**
- **Fever:** Cytokines reset hypothalamic set‑point.
- **Tachypnoea:** Direct cytokine effect on respiratory centre + metabolic acidosis (lactic acid from anaerobic metabolism) → compensatory hyperventilation.
- **Tachycardia:** Sympathetic drive due to vasodilation, hypovolaemia, and cytokine‑mediated myocardial depression (sepsis cardiomyopathy).
- **Hypotension:** Cytokines induce widespread vasodilation (nitric oxide) → relative hypovolaemia; plus capillary leak → absolute hypovolaemia; plus myocardial depression reduces cardiac output.
- **Cool peripheries, prolonged capillary refill:** Vasoconstriction in skin as blood is shunted to vital organs (compensated shock) – “cold” shock pattern.
- **Confusion/drowsiness:** Cerebral hypoperfusion + mitochondrial dysfunction + direct neuroinflammation.
- **Oliguria/anuria:** Renal hypoperfusion and microvascular thrombosis → acute tubular necrosis.
- **Elevated lactate:** Tissue hypoxia → anaerobic glycolysis; also cytokine‑driven increased aerobic glycolysis (stress response). Lactate >2 mmol/L is a marker of hypoperfusion, but note lactate can be high in sepsis without shock (e.g., liver failure). In sepsis, lactate clearance guides resuscitation.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**  
- The vignette patient had “cold” shock: suggests late decompensation, often seen in elderly with poor cardiac reserve.  
- “Warm” shock (vasoplegic) is early with high cardiac output – better prognosis if caught before myocardial dysfunction develops.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Causes | Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Pulmonary** | Pneumonia (Strep. pneumoniae, Staph. aureus, Gram‑negatives) | Productive cough, fever, lobar consolidation on CXR |
| **Abdominal** | Perforated viscus, cholecystitis, cholangitis, diverticulitis, appendicitis, post‑op leak | Abdominal pain, guarding, jaundice, bilious NG output |
| **Urinary** | Pyelonephritis, complicated UTI, post‑procedural (stent) | Loin pain, dysuria, positive urine dip, recent instrumentation |
| **Skin & soft tissue** | Cellulitis, necrotising fasciitis, wound infection (esp. surgical) | Erythema, swelling, crepitus, blistering, pain out of proportion |
| **Line‑associated** | Central line, dialysis catheter, peripheral cannula | Redness/tenderness at line site, recent line days |
| **Intravascular** | Endocarditis (Staph aureus, Strep viridans) | Cardiac murmur, splinter haemorrhages, Janeway lesions, persistent bacteraemia |
| **Other** | Meningitis (meningococcal, pneumococcal), obstetric (chorioamnionitis), immunosuppressed (aspergillosis, CMV) | Neck stiffness, petechial rash, pregnancy, neutropaenia |

**Most common cause:** Community‑acquired pneumonia remains the leading cause of sepsis, but in hospitalised patients consider intravascular catheters and surgical wounds.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria – qSOFA (quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment):** Altered mental status + respiratory rate ≥22/min + systolic BP ≤100 mmHg = 2 criteria → high risk of adverse outcome. **Septic shock** defined as sepsis with persisting hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg and lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation (Sepsis‑3 definition).

**Key tests:**
- **Lactate** – what: marker of hypoperfusion. Why: guide resuscitation, stratify severity. Interpret: >2 mmol/L = tissue hypoperfusion; >4 mmol/L = severe shock. Serial lactate clearance is prognostic (goal: ↓ by ≥20% in 6 hours).
- **Blood cultures (2 sets)** – what: identify pathogen. Why: targeted antibiotics. Interpret: positive = confirm infection; negative doesn’t rule out (prior antibiotics, fastidious organisms). Always take before antibiotics if possible (delay ≤45 min).
- **Full blood count** – leucocytosis or leucopenia; thrombocytopenia occurs in DIC.
- **C‑reactive protein / procalcitonin** – inflammatory markers; procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL supports bacterial infection; useful to guide de‑escalation.
- **Urea & creatinine** – renal function; AKI defined by ↑ creatinine ≥1.5× baseline or urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 h.
- **Coagulation screen** – DIC (↑ PT/APTT, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ D‑dimer).
- **Arterial blood gas** – pH, PaO₂, PaCO₂, lactate, bicarbonate. For shock: lactate, base deficit, and PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio (ARDS).
- **Imaging:** CXR (pneumonia), CT abdomen/pelvis if source unclear, ultrasound (FAST, echo) for effusion, abscess, endocarditis.
- **Urine dip and culture** – if UTI suspected.
- **Consider: liver function tests, bedside USS for volume status (IVC collapsibility), echo (sepsis cardiomyopathy).**

**Pattern‑recognition:** A single finding that **confirms** septic shock = **persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors after adequate fluid resuscitation + lactate >2 mmol/L**.  
**Ruling out** – a normal lactate (<2) and no hypotension after fluids → not shock (but may still have sepsis).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour – the “Sepsis Six”):**
1. **Oxygen** – give high‑flow O₂ to target SpO₂ ≥94%. Why: improve tissue oxygenation.
2. **Blood cultures** – two sets (aerobic/anaerobic) before antibiotics (ideally). Why: identify pathogen.
3. **Bloods** – lactate, FBC, U&E, CRP, coagulation, LFT.
4. **IV fluids** – start with 30 mL/kg crystalloid (e.g., 0.9% saline or balanced solution like Hartmann’s) over 15–30 min. Why: correct hypovolaemia. But reassess for fluid overload (lung crackles, JVP).
5. **IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics** – **within 1 hour**: e.g., piperacillin‑tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 8 h (or ceftriaxone 2 g IV ± metronidazole 500 mg IV if intra‑abdominal). Modify based on source and local guidelines.
6. **Urine output monitoring** – catheterise. Why: guide fluid resuscitation, detect AKI.

**After first hour:**
- **Vasopressors** if MAP <65 mmHg despite adequate fluid (≥30 mL/kg). First‑line: **norepinephrine** (titrate 0.01–3 µg/kg/min IV). Why: α‑agonist increases peripheral resistance. If refractory, add **vasopressin** (0.03 U/min) or **epinephrine**.
- **Central line** – for reliable vasopressor delivery and monitoring central venous pressure (CVP, dynamic if possible) and ScvO₂.
- **Repeat lactate** every 2–4 h to guide resuscitation. Goal: lactate clearance.
- **Source control** – urgently: drain abscess, remove infected catheter, debride necrotising fasciitis, operate on perforation.

**Ward (24–72 h):**
- **Monitor haemodynamics** – MAP ≥65, urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/h, lactate falling.
- **ICU input** – if on vasopressors or lactate >4, admit to ICU/HDU for invasive monitoring.
- **Antibiotic de‑escalation** – when cultures return, narrow spectrum. Stop double Gram‑negative cover if possible.
- **Corticosteroids** – consider **hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q6h** if shock persists despite fluids and vasopressors (randomised trial evidence: reduces time on vasopressors). Side effects: hyperglycaemia, immunosuppression.
- **Tight glucose control** – insulin infusion targeting 6–10 mmol/L. Hyperglycaemia worsens outcomes.
- **Nutrition** – start enteral feeding as soon as haemodynamically stable.

**Long‑term:**
- **Rehabilitation** – critical illness polyneuropathy, muscle wasting. Physiotherapy, nutrition.
- **Vaccination** – pneumococcal, influenza if indicated.
- **Surveillance** – recurrence risk (e.g., recurrent UTI, recurrent line infections). Address modifiable risk factors.

**What NOT to do (pitfalls):**
- **Do not** delay antibiotics >1 hour. “Start smart, then focus.”
- **Do not** fluid‑overload. After initial 30 mL/kg, switch to dynamic assessment (passive leg raise, stroke volume variation).
- **Do not** use dopamine as first‑line vasopressor (increased arrhythmias vs noradrenaline).
- **Do not** give hydrocortisone routinely – only if vasopressor‑dependent after adequate fluid resuscitation.
- **Do not** forget source control – the most modifiable factor after antibiotics.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Septic Shock | Anaphylactic Shock | Hypovolaemic Shock | Cardiogenic Shock |
|---------|--------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Pain** | Variable (by source) | None typical (urticaria, wheeze) | No pain unless trauma/haemorrhage | Chest pain (MI), dyspnoea |
| **Exam** | Fever, warm or cold peripheries, wound/infection signs | Urticaria, angioedema, wheeze, stridor, hypotension | Cool, clammy, collapsed veins, dry mucous membranes | Raised JVP, crackles, S3 gallop, cool peripheries |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia, non‑specific ST changes | Sinus tachycardia | Sinus tachycardia | ST elevation (MI), arrhythmia, left ventricular hypertrophy |
| **Labs** | ↑ Lactate, ↑ WBC, ↑ CRP, positive cultures | ↓ Serum tryptase (acute) | ↑ Lactate, ↓ Hb (if haemorrhage no ↑ haematocrit) | ↑ Troponin, ↑ BNP, ↑ Lactate |
| **Key differentiator** | Infection source + qSOFA ≥2 + persistent hypotension after fluids | Acute onset with allergen exposure; resolves with adrenaline | No signs of infection/anaphylaxis; recent bleeding / fluid loss | Signs of pump failure; raised JVP, pulmonary oedema on CXR |

**Mimics to keep in mind:**  
- **Pancreatitis** – SIRS, fever, hypotension, but amylase/lipase elevated and CT findings.  
- **Adrenal crisis** – shock, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, pigmentation, previous steroids. Give IV hydrocortisone urgently.  
- **Pulmonary embolism** – shock with raised JVP, hypoxia, but no fever/infection; ECG S1Q3T3, elevated D‑dimer, CT pulmonary angiogram.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS):** 
  - **How presents:** Hypoxia (PaO₂/FiO₂ <300), bilateral infiltrates on CXR, no signs of left atrial hypertension.
  - **Key signs:** Increasing O₂ requirements, frothy sputum, worsening tachypnoea.
  - **What to do:** Lung‑protective ventilation (tidal volume 6 mL/kg ideal body weight, plateau pressure <30 cmH₂O), consider prone positioning, neuromuscular blockade.
- **Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC):**
  - **Presentation:** Bleeding from venipuncture sites, petechiae, purpura, or thrombosis (e.g., digital ischaemia).
  - **Key labs:** ↓ platelets, ↑ PT/APTT, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ D‑dimer.
  - **Management:** Treat underlying sepsis; platelet transfusion if bleeding or ≤10×10⁹/L; cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.5 g/L; no routine heparin.
- **Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) requiring RRT:**
  - **Presentation:** Oliguria/anuria, rising creatinine >176 µmol/L, hyperkalaemia, metabolic acidosis, fluid overload.
  - **Key signs:** Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for >6 h, creatinine doubling.
  - **What to do:** Stop nephrotoxic drugs; consider renal replacement therapy (CVVHDF or haemodialysis) early if refractory.
- **Multi‑organ dysfunction (MODS):** 
  - Sequential failure of two or more organs. High mortality.
  - **Late presentation:** patient remains hypotensive despite high‑dose vasopressors, anuric, ventilator‑dependent, coagulopathic. Need full ICU support.

**Late‑presentation patterns in exams:** A patient with “flu‑like symptoms” who presents 5 days later with confusion, low BP, petechial rash – think meningococcal sepsis, which can rapidly progress to purpura fulminans and limb ischaemia. Must give IV ceftriaxone immediately (even before cultures if spinal) and contact ICU.
sections.9. High‑Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **SEPSIS SIX** (for immediate management – also QI bundle):
   - **S** – Send blood cultures + full bloods  
   - **E** – Early broad‑spectrum antibiotics  
   - **P** – Push IV fluids (30 mL/kg)  
   - **S** – Source control  
   - **I** – Invasive monitoring (CSL, urine output)  
   - **S** – Support (O₂, vasopressors)

2. **HOT HOT RED** (for signs of septic shock):
   - **H** – Hypotension (MAP <65)  
   - **O** – Oliguria  
   - **T** – Tachypnoea  
   - **H** – Hypoxia  
   - **O** – Obtundation (confusion)  
   - **T** – Tachycardia  
   - **R** – Raised lactate  
   - **E** – Elevated CRP/WBC  
   - **D** – DIC / dysfunction

**Pearls:**
- **Cold shock vs warm shock:** Cold (vasoconstricted, narrow pulse pressure) suggests additional hypovolaemia or cardiac dysfunction; warm (vasodilated, bounding pulses) is classic early sepsis. Manage both with fluids first, but cold shock may require inotrope (e.g., dobutamine) if ongoing low cardiac output.
- **Lactate ≠ anaerobic only:** In sepsis, up to 50% of lactate comes from increased aerobic glycolysis (adrenergic stimulation). Still, elevated lactate correlates with severity.
- **Cortisol and vasopressor dependence:** If a patient needs >0.5 µg/kg/min noradrenaline, consider “relative adrenal insufficiency” – give hydrocortisone 200 mg daily.
- **Do not use albumin for acute fluid resuscitation** unless patient is cirrhotic (evidence shows no benefit vs crystalloid).
- **Initial fluid rate:** The “30 mL/kg” is a starting point; in patients with heart failure, be cautious – use smaller boluses (250 mL) with frequent reassessment.
- **The single most important factor reducing mortality is TIME** – antibiotics ≤1 hour, source control ≤12 h.
- **NEWS2 (National Early Warning Score):** A NEWS ≥7 (e.g., RR 25, SpO₂ 89%, HR 110, SBP 90, alert, temp 38.5) triggers immediate team response – do not ignore.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- **Missing hypothermia** in the elderly as a sign of infection.
- **Not repeating cultures** if no clinical response after 48–72h – could be fungal or resistant organism.
- **Holding vasopressors** because of “low BP still” – pressors are life‑saving; don’t wait for central line.
- **Not stopping antibiotics** when cultures are negative – leads to secondary infections (C. difficile, Candida).
- **Forgetting to decontaminate line** – remove suspect catheters (e.g., CVL days >5).
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**Immediate escalation to senior/ICU (within 15–30 minutes if any of the following are present):**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **MAP after fluids** | <65 mmHg despite 30 mL/kg crystalloid | Start noradrenaline, call ICU |
| **Lactate** | >4 mmol/L on arterial blood gas | Escalate to ICU/HDU |
| **NEWS2** | ≥7 (or any single red parameter: RR ≥25, SpO₂ ≤91%, HR ≥131, SBP ≤90, GCS <15) | Immediate MDT review |
| **GCS** | Drop ≥2 points or GCS <13 | Urgent CT head if no other cause; critical care referral |
| **Urine output** | <0.5 mL/kg/h for >2 h despite fluid resus | Acute kidney injury, consider ICU for RRT |
| **Unstable arrhythmia** | Atrial fibrillation with rapid VR, ventricular tachycardia | Call cardiac arrest team if peri‑arrest, else ICU |
| **Respiratory failure** | SpO₂ <92% despite 15 L O₂, or PaO₂/FiO₂ <200 | Non‑invasive ventilation or intubation; ICU referral |
| **Suspected source requiring surgery** | e.g., abdominal catastrophe, necrotising fasciitis | Call general surgery / orthopaedics emergently; ICU for peri‑op |

**Negative de‑escalation:** If the patient stabilises – lactate falling, MAP ≥65 on minimal/no vasopressors, no new organ failures – can step down to ward with track‑and‑trigger. But always discuss with senior before leaving ICU/HDU.

## Septic Shock
**One-liner:** A life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection, requiring immediate recognition and aggressive intervention.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
You are bleeped to see a 72-year-old man on the orthopaedic ward, three days post‑hip hemiarthroplasty. The nurse says he “looks off” and his blood pressure is 82/45. On arrival, he is drowsy but rousable, breathing at 28/min, heart rate 112 bpm, temperature 38.9°C, SaO₂ 90% on room air. His right thigh wound is erythematous and weeping serous fluid. Capillary refill is 5 seconds, peripheries are cool. He has been anuric for 6 hours.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Hypotension (82/45)** → inadequate perfusion pressure → septic shock (not just sepsis) – need vasopressors after fluid.
- **Drowsy/confusion** → cerebral hypoperfusion or sepsis-associated encephalopathy.
- **Tachypnoea (28) + hypoxia (90%)** → ARDS or compensatory respiratory alkalosis ± metabolic acidosis.
- **Tachycardia (112)** → compensatory response to vasodilation and hypovolaemia.
- **Fever (38.9°C)** → acute phase response; but note hypothermia can also occur in severe sepsis.
- **Wound infection** → source – likely Gram‑positive (Staph aureus/Strep) or mixed.
- **Prolonged capillary refill, cool peripheries** → peripheral vasoconstriction suggests a “cold” shock pattern (often hypovolaemic or cardiogenic component).
- **Anuria** → acute kidney injury from hypoperfusion.

**Atypical presentations:**
1. **“Warm” septic shock** – young female with pyelonephritis: hypotensive but bounding pulses, warm peripheries, capillary refill <2s (vasoplegic shock early phase).
2. **Hypothermic sepsis** – elderly male with urosepsis: temp 35.5°C, lethargic, no fever – common in immunocompromised or very old.
3. **Occult abdominal source** – post‑op patient with ileus, mild abdominal distension, rising lactate – no obvious peritonitis. E.g., anastomotic leak.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Plain‑language definition:** Sepsis is your body’s overwhelming inflammatory response to an infection; septic shock is when that response causes blood pressure to drop so low that organs are starved of oxygen – it’s the deadliest form.

**Why it matters:**  
- **Frequency:** >11 million sepsis‑related deaths globally per year; in UK hospitals, ~260,000 cases annually with ~48,000 deaths.
- **Stakes:** Mortality for septic shock ranges 40–60% if not recognised immediately; every hour delay in antibiotics increases mortality by ~8%.
- **Common pitfalls:** Missing the diagnosis in the “warm” phase; fluid‑overloading the patient with poor ventricular function; not escalating to ICU early; failing to re‑source control.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings
The story begins with a pathogen (bacteria, virus, fungus, parasite) entering the body. That pathogen triggers pattern‑recognition receptors (Toll‑like receptors, etc.) on immune cells → massive release of pro‑inflammatory cytokines (TNF‑α, IL‑1, IL‑6).

**Why each sign occurs:**
- **Fever:** Cytokines reset hypothalamic set‑point.
- **Tachypnoea:** Direct cytokine effect on respiratory centre + metabolic acidosis (lactic acid from anaerobic metabolism) → compensatory hyperventilation.
- **Tachycardia:** Sympathetic drive due to vasodilation, hypovolaemia, and cytokine‑mediated myocardial depression (sepsis cardiomyopathy).
- **Hypotension:** Cytokines induce widespread vasodilation (nitric oxide) → relative hypovolaemia; plus capillary leak → absolute hypovolaemia; plus myocardial depression reduces cardiac output.
- **Cool peripheries, prolonged capillary refill:** Vasoconstriction in skin as blood is shunted to vital organs (compensated shock) – “cold” shock pattern.
- **Confusion/drowsiness:** Cerebral hypoperfusion + mitochondrial dysfunction + direct neuroinflammation.
- **Oliguria/anuria:** Renal hypoperfusion and microvascular thrombosis → acute tubular necrosis.
- **Elevated lactate:** Tissue hypoxia → anaerobic glycolysis; also cytokine‑driven increased aerobic glycolysis (stress response). Lactate >2 mmol/L is a marker of hypoperfusion, but note lactate can be high in sepsis without shock (e.g., liver failure). In sepsis, lactate clearance guides resuscitation.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**  
- The vignette patient had “cold” shock: suggests late decompensation, often seen in elderly with poor cardiac reserve.  
- “Warm” shock (vasoplegic) is early with high cardiac output – better prognosis if caught before myocardial dysfunction develops.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Common Causes | Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Pulmonary** | Pneumonia (Strep. pneumoniae, Staph. aureus, Gram‑negatives) | Productive cough, fever, lobar consolidation on CXR |
| **Abdominal** | Perforated viscus, cholecystitis, cholangitis, diverticulitis, appendicitis, post‑op leak | Abdominal pain, guarding, jaundice, bilious NG output |
| **Urinary** | Pyelonephritis, complicated UTI, post‑procedural (stent) | Loin pain, dysuria, positive urine dip, recent instrumentation |
| **Skin & soft tissue** | Cellulitis, necrotising fasciitis, wound infection (esp. surgical) | Erythema, swelling, crepitus, blistering, pain out of proportion |
| **Line‑associated** | Central line, dialysis catheter, peripheral cannula | Redness/tenderness at line site, recent line days |
| **Intravascular** | Endocarditis (Staph aureus, Strep viridans) | Cardiac murmur, splinter haemorrhages, Janeway lesions, persistent bacteraemia |
| **Other** | Meningitis (meningococcal, pneumococcal), obstetric (chorioamnionitis), immunosuppressed (aspergillosis, CMV) | Neck stiffness, petechial rash, pregnancy, neutropaenia |

**Most common cause:** Community‑acquired pneumonia remains the leading cause of sepsis, but in hospitalised patients consider intravascular catheters and surgical wounds.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria – qSOFA (quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment):** Altered mental status + respiratory rate ≥22/min + systolic BP ≤100 mmHg = 2 criteria → high risk of adverse outcome. **Septic shock** defined as sepsis with persisting hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg and lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation (Sepsis‑3 definition).

**Key tests:**
- **Lactate** – what: marker of hypoperfusion. Why: guide resuscitation, stratify severity. Interpret: >2 mmol/L = tissue hypoperfusion; >4 mmol/L = severe shock. Serial lactate clearance is prognostic (goal: ↓ by ≥20% in 6 hours).
- **Blood cultures (2 sets)** – what: identify pathogen. Why: targeted antibiotics. Interpret: positive = confirm infection; negative doesn’t rule out (prior antibiotics, fastidious organisms). Always take before antibiotics if possible (delay ≤45 min).
- **Full blood count** – leucocytosis or leucopenia; thrombocytopenia occurs in DIC.
- **C‑reactive protein / procalcitonin** – inflammatory markers; procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL supports bacterial infection; useful to guide de‑escalation.
- **Urea & creatinine** – renal function; AKI defined by ↑ creatinine ≥1.5× baseline or urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 h.
- **Coagulation screen** – DIC (↑ PT/APTT, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ D‑dimer).
- **Arterial blood gas** – pH, PaO₂, PaCO₂, lactate, bicarbonate. For shock: lactate, base deficit, and PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio (ARDS).
- **Imaging:** CXR (pneumonia), CT abdomen/pelvis if source unclear, ultrasound (FAST, echo) for effusion, abscess, endocarditis.
- **Urine dip and culture** – if UTI suspected.
- **Consider: liver function tests, bedside USS for volume status (IVC collapsibility), echo (sepsis cardiomyopathy).**

**Pattern‑recognition:** A single finding that **confirms** septic shock = **persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors after adequate fluid resuscitation + lactate >2 mmol/L**.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour – the “Sepsis Six”):**
1. **Oxygen** – give high‑flow O₂ to target SpO₂ ≥94%. Why: improve tissue oxygenation.
2. **Blood cultures** – two sets (aerobic/anaerobic) before antibiotics (ideally). Why: identify pathogen.
3. **Bloods** – lactate, FBC, U&E, CRP, coagulation, LFT.
4. **IV fluids** – start with 30 mL/kg crystalloid (e.g., 0.9% saline or balanced solution like Hartmann’s) over 15–30 min. Why: correct hypovolaemia. But reassess for fluid overload (lung crackles, JVP).
5. **IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics** – **within 1 hour**: e.g., piperacillin‑tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 8 h (or ceftriaxone 2 g IV ± metronidazole 500 mg IV if intra‑abdominal). Modify based on source and local guidelines.
6. **Urine output monitoring** – catheterise. Why: guide fluid resuscitation, detect AKI.

**After first hour:**
- **Vasopressors** if MAP <65 mmHg despite adequate fluid (≥30 mL/kg). First‑line: **norepinephrine** (titrate 0.01–3 µg/kg/min IV). Why: α‑agonist increases peripheral resistance. If refractory, add **vasopressin** (0.03 U/min) or **epinephrine**.
- **Central line** – for reliable vasopressor delivery and monitoring central venous pressure (CVP, dynamic if possible) and ScvO₂.
- **Repeat lactate** every 2–4 h to guide resuscitation. Goal: lactate clearance.
- **Source control** – urgently: drain abscess, remove infected catheter, debride necrotising fasciitis, operate on perforation.

**Ward (24–72 h):**
- **Monitor haemodynamics** – MAP ≥65, urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/h, lactate falling.
- **ICU input** – if on vasopressors or lactate >4, admit to ICU/HDU for invasive monitoring.
- **Antibiotic de‑escalation** – when cultures return, narrow spectrum. Stop double Gram‑negative cover if possible.
- **Corticosteroids** – consider **hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q6h** if shock persists despite fluids and vasopressors (randomised trial evidence: reduces time on vasopressors). Side effects: hyperglycaemia, immunosuppression.
- **Tight glucose control** – insulin infusion targeting 6–10 mmol/L. Hyperglycaemia worsens outcomes.
- **Nutrition** – start enteral feeding as soon as haemodynamically stable.

**Long‑term:**
- **Rehabilitation** – critical illness polyneuropathy, muscle wasting. Physiotherapy, nutrition.
- **Vaccination** – pneumococcal, influenza if indicated.
- **Surveillance** – recurrence risk (e.g., recurrent UTI, recurrent line infections). Address modifiable risk factors.

**What NOT to do (pitfalls):**
- **Do not** delay antibiotics >1 hour. “Start smart, then focus.”
- **Do not** fluid‑overload. After initial 30 mL/kg, switch to dynamic assessment (passive leg raise, stroke volume variation).
- **Do not** use dopamine as first‑line vasopressor (increased arrhythmias vs noradrenaline).
- **Do not** give hydrocortisone routinely – only if vasopressor‑dependent after adequate fluid resuscitation.
- **Do not** forget source control – the most modifiable factor after antibiotics.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | Septic Shock | Anaphylactic Shock | Hypovolaemic Shock | Cardiogenic Shock |
|---------|--------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Pain** | Variable (by source) | None typical (urticaria, wheeze) | No pain unless trauma/haemorrhage | Chest pain (MI), dyspnoea |
| **Exam** | Fever, warm or cold peripheries, wound/infection signs | Urticaria, angioedema, wheeze, stridor, hypotension | Cool, clammy, collapsed veins, dry mucous membranes | Raised JVP, crackles, S3 gallop, cool peripheries |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia, non‑specific ST changes | Sinus tachycardia | Sinus tachycardia | ST elevation (MI), arrhythmia, left ventricular hypertrophy |
| **Labs** | ↑ Lactate, ↑ WBC, ↑ CRP, positive cultures | ↓ Serum tryptase (acute) | ↑ Lactate, ↓ Hb (if haemorrhage no ↑ haematocrit) | ↑ Troponin, ↑ BNP, ↑ Lactate |
| **Key differentiator** | Infection source + qSOFA ≥2 + persistent hypotension after fluids | Acute onset with allergen exposure; resolves with adrenaline | No signs of infection/anaphylaxis; recent bleeding / fluid loss | Signs of pump failure; raised JVP, pulmonary oedema on CXR |

**Mimics to keep in mind:**  
- **Pancreatitis** – SIRS, fever, hypotension, but amylase/lipase elevated and CT findings.  
- **Adrenal crisis** – shock, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, pigmentation, previous steroids. Give IV hydrocortisone urgently.  
- **Pulmonary embolism** – shock with raised JVP, hypoxia, but no fever/infection; ECG S1Q3T3, elevated D‑dimer, CT pulmonary angiogram.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
- **Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS):** 
  - **How presents:** Hypoxia (PaO₂/FiO₂ <300), bilateral infiltrates on CXR, no signs of left atrial hypertension.
  - **Key signs:** Increasing O₂ requirements, frothy sputum, worsening tachypnoea.
  - **What to do:** Lung‑protective ventilation (tidal volume 6 mL/kg ideal body weight, plateau pressure <30 cmH₂O), consider prone positioning, neuromuscular blockade.
- **Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC):**
  - **Presentation:** Bleeding from venipuncture sites, petechiae, purpura, or thrombosis (e.g., digital ischaemia).
  - **Key labs:** ↓ platelets, ↑ PT/APTT, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ D‑dimer.
  - **Management:** Treat underlying sepsis; platelet transfusion if bleeding or ≤10×10⁹/L; cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.5 g/L; no routine heparin.
- **Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) requiring RRT:**
  - **Presentation:** Oliguria/anuria, rising creatinine >176 µmol/L, hyperkalaemia, metabolic acidosis, fluid overload.
  - **Key signs:** Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for >6 h, creatinine doubling.
  - **What to do:** Stop nephrotoxic drugs; consider renal replacement therapy (CVVHDF or haemodialysis) early if refractory.
- **Multi‑organ dysfunction (MODS):** 
  - Sequential failure of two or more organs. High mortality.
  - **Late presentation:** patient remains hypotensive despite high‑dose vasopressors, anuric, ventilator‑dependent, coagulopathic. Need full ICU support.

**Late‑presentation patterns in exams:** A patient with “flu‑like symptoms” who presents 5 days later with confusion, low BP, petechial rash – think meningococcal sepsis, which can rapidly progress to purpura fulminans and limb ischaemia. Must give IV ceftriaxone immediately (even before cultures if spinal) and contact ICU.

### 9. High‑Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**

1. **SEPSIS SIX** (for immediate management – also QI bundle):
   - **S** – Send blood cultures + full bloods  
   - **E** – Early broad‑spectrum antibiotics  
   - **P** – Push IV fluids (30 mL/kg)  
   - **S** – Source control  
   - **I** – Invasive monitoring (CSL, urine output)  
   - **S** – Support (O₂, vasopressors)

2. **HOT HOT RED** (for signs of septic shock):
   - **H** – Hypotension (MAP <65)  
   - **O** – Oliguria  
   - **T** – Tachypnoea  
   - **H** – Hypoxia  
   - **O** – Obtundation (confusion)  
   - **T** – Tachycardia  
   - **R** – Raised lactate  
   - **E** – Elevated CRP/WBC  
   - **D** – DIC / dysfunction

**Pearls:**
- **Cold shock vs warm shock:** Cold (vasoconstricted, narrow pulse pressure) suggests additional hypovolaemia or cardiac dysfunction; warm (vasodilated, bounding pulses) is classic early sepsis. Manage both with fluids first, but cold shock may require inotrope (e.g., dobutamine) if ongoing low cardiac output.
- **Lactate ≠ anaerobic only:** In sepsis, up to 50% of lactate comes from increased aerobic glycolysis (adrenergic stimulation). Still, elevated lactate correlates with severity.
- **Cortisol and vasopressor dependence:** If a patient needs >0.5 µg/kg/min noradrenaline, consider “relative adrenal insufficiency” – give hydrocortisone 200 mg daily.
- **Do not use albumin for acute fluid resuscitation** unless patient is cirrhotic (evidence shows no benefit vs crystalloid).
- **Initial fluid rate:** The “30 mL/kg” is a starting point; in patients with heart failure, be cautious – use smaller boluses (250 mL) with frequent reassessment.
- **The single most important factor reducing mortality is TIME** – antibiotics ≤1 hour, source control ≤12 h.
- **NEWS2 (National Early Warning Score):** A NEWS ≥7 (e.g., RR 25, SpO₂ 89%, HR 110, SBP 90, alert, temp 38.5) triggers immediate team response – do not ignore.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- **Missing hypothermia** in the elderly as a sign of infection.
- **Not repeating cultures** if no clinical response after 48–72h – could be fungal or resistant organism.
- **Holding vasopressors** because of “low BP still” – pressors are life‑saving; don’t wait for central line.
- **Not stopping antibiotics** when cultures are negative – leads to secondary infections (C. difficile, Candida).
- **Forgetting to decontaminate line** – remove suspect catheters (e.g., CVL days >5).

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**Immediate escalation to senior/ICU (within 15–30 minutes if any of the following are present):**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **MAP after fluids** | <65 mmHg despite 30 mL/kg crystalloid | Start noradrenaline, call ICU |
| **Lactate** | >4 mmol/L on arterial blood gas | Escalate to ICU/HDU |
| **NEWS2** | ≥7 (or any single red parameter: RR ≥25, SpO₂ ≤91%, HR ≥131, SBP ≤90, GCS <15) | Immediate MDT review |
| **GCS** | Drop ≥2 points or GCS <13 | Urgent CT head if no other cause; critical care referral |
| **Urine output** | <0.5 mL/kg/h for >2 h despite fluid resus | Acute kidney injury, consider ICU for RRT |
| **Unstable arrhythmia** | Atrial fibrillation with rapid VR, ventricular tachycardia | Call cardiac arrest team if peri‑arrest, else ICU |
| **Respiratory failure** | SpO₂ <92% despite 15 L O₂, or PaO₂/FiO₂ <200 | Non‑invasive ventilation or intubation; ICU referral |
| **Suspected source requiring surgery** | e.g., abdominal catastrophe, necrotising fasciitis | Call general surgery / orthopaedics emergently; ICU for peri‑op |

**Negative de‑escalation:** If the patient stabilises – lactate falling, MAP ≥65 on minimal/no vasopressors, no new organ failures – can step down to ward with track‑and‑trigger. But always discuss with senior before leaving ICU/HDU.
